data.title: Miran says Trump didn't pressure him on how to vote at FOMC meeting
data.description:
Stephen Miran, who was on the White House staff at the start of this week, said as a Federal Reserve governor, "I will do my own independent analysis, based on my interpretation of the data."

He spoke to President Donald Trump once since he was sworn in shortly before the Federal Open Market Committee meeting on Tuesday. The president called to congratulate him and didn't ask him to take any specific actions, Miran said on CNBC.

Miran was the only FOMC member to dissent from the Fed's decision to cut its benchmark rate by 25 basis points. Instead, he was pushing for a 50-bp cut.

He took an unpaid leave of absence from the Council of Economic Advisers when the was sworn in as a Fed governor on Tuesday. That has raised criticism from Democrats, who see it as a conflict of interest.

All Fed members were welcoming at the meeting this week, he said, including Lisa Cook, who President Trump is trying to remove from the central bank.

Miran isn't seeing "any material inflation from tariffs," but "I'm clearly in the minority" in assessing the effect of tariffs on inflation, he added. One indicator backing his view is that "there's no discernable trend difference between core goods inflation in the United States vs. that in other countries." 

He expects that less immigration and the immigrants leaving the U.S. will have a disinflationary effect, as there will be less demand, especially for housing.

The new FOMC member provided the most dovish outlook in the Summary of Economic Projections' dot plot, where he projected the federal funds rate at between 2.75% and 3.00% at the end of 2025. Miran said he'll deliver a speech on Monday that will give "a full accounting" of his economic views, laying out the math for how he gets to those numbers.

If the interest rate goes down, then government debt cost will decline, he noted. But Miran said he'll focus on the Fed's dual mandate of full employment and stable prices.

When asked if the Fed should use its balance sheet to bring down mortgage rates, "I don't think it's appropriate for the Fed to be involved in credit allocation," he said.

data.title: Brighthouse Financial gains after report of Aquarian $65-$70/share offer
data.description:
Brighthouse Financial (NASDAQ:BHF) rose 6% after a report that a consortium led by insurer Aquarian Holdings is in advanced discussions to pay $65 to $70 a share for Brighthouse.

Brighthouse (NASDAQ:BHF) may decide as soon as next week to go forward with a sale or to remain a standalone company, according to a Bloomberg report on  Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter. Aquarian is working with co-investors including Mubadala Capital and the Qatar Investment Authority.

Brighthouse declined to comment to Bloomberg. Aquarian didn’t immediately respond to request for comment. 

The latest update comes after the Financial Times reported earlier this month that Aquarian was said to be in late-stage discussions  with investors for financing its takeover of the Brighthouse (BHF).

data.title: Market Voices: Xi on TikTok, Fed's Kashkari, SEC chair on rule change
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements and remarks that could impact markets, sectors or individual stocks.

  • Chinese President Xi Jinping told President Donald Trump during a call on Friday to discuss trade policy that his government was open to negotiating over TikTok, but didn't indicate a deal had already been reached.

 "On TikTok, Xi said China's position is clear: the Chinese government respects corporate will and welcomes companies to conduct business negotiations on the basis of market rules to reach a solution consistent with Chinese laws and regulations and a balance of interests," according to Reuters, which cited Chinese state media outlet Xinhua. 

TikTok is owned by China's ByteDance (BDNCE). 

  • Minneapolis Federal Reserve President Neel Kashkari said he doesn't expect tariffs to significantly pressure inflation long term, adding that he hopes to see the Fed vote for additional rate cuts at its too remaining meetings this year.  

"It really comes down to, do you believe tariffs are a one-time effect or something more persistent?” Kashkari said during an interview with CNBC on Friday. “I’m getting more confident that it’s likely a one-time effect, but it’s going to take a couple years for it to play out.” 

Kashkari is not currently a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee, but will become one next year, CNBC added. 

  • SEC Chairman Paul Atkins said he plans to propose that companies be given the option of filing financial reports biannually rather than quarterly. 

Atkin's announcement comes in the wake of comments by President Trump that quarterly reporting was an unnecessary drain on company resources. 

“I welcome that posting by the president, and I have talked to him about it,” Atkins told CNBC on Friday. “In principle, I think to propose change in what our rules are now, I think would be a good way forward, and then we’ll consider that and move forward after that.” 

If the SEC approves the change, it would be up to individual companies to decide whether they want to report quarterly or biannually, he added. 

“For the sake of shareholders and public companies, the market can decide what the proper cadence is,” Atkins said.

data.title: Quantum computing stocks rise to end action-packed week
data.description:
Quantum computing stocks rose on Friday to end an action-packed week in the sector. 

Leading the gains were Quantum Co. (NASDAQ:QMCO), which rose more than 28% in late morning trading, and Quantum Computing (NASDAQ:QUBT), which added more than 22%. Also seeing strong gains were IonQ (NYSE:IONQ), Arqit Quantum (NASDAQ:ARQQ) and D-Wave Quantum (QBTS), all of which rose 3% or more. Rigetti Computing (RGTI) added nearly 11%, shortly before 12 p.m. EST.

It was unclear what was driving the moves higher in the sector, though investor interest in the space has been renewed with vigor in 2025, amid advancements and tech giants such as Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT) and Nvidia (NVDA) showing an interest.

Earlier this week, IonQ, Honeywell (HON) and The Electric Power Board of Chattanooga partnered with the U.S. Department of Energy to boost quantum computing use cases in space. IonQ also said it was acquiring Vector Atomic to expand into quantum sensing.

data.title: Indigenous chief threatens Teck-Anglo merger deal
data.description:
A First Nations leader in Canada's British Columbia said Thursday he is concerned the proposed merger between Teck Resources (NYSE:TECK) and Anglo American (OTCQX:AAUKF) (OTCQX:NGLOY) will ignore indigenous rights and cannot be completed without indigenous inclusion.

Chief Clarence Louie of the Osooyos Indian Band said he would bring political and legal challenges unless the companies consult with them over Teck's (NYSE:TECK) expansion plans for the Trail smelter in southern British Columbia.

Teck (TECK) and Anglo (OTCQX:AAUKF) (OTCQX:NGLOY) have committed ~$4.5B in spending in Canada over five years as part of the proposed merger, and a significant portion already has been announced by Teck, including as much as $750M to add copper and zinc refining to the Trail smelter, which currently handles zinc and lead.

Louie said Teck (TECK) has not meaningfully talked to the Osooyos group about the proposed merger and investments for the Trail operations, which have faced past complaints about pollution.

The indigenous community has "experienced significant impacts without any benefit" during the century the smelter has operated, Louie said, calling the proposal to expand the operations without consulting First Nations "unconscionable."

Louie said efforts are underway to meet Teck (TECK) CEO Jonathan Price within weeks, after a previous meeting was canceled.

data.title: AMC Entertainment reportedly collaborating with Taylor Swift for her 'secret' theatrical event
data.description:
According to a Friday report by The Hollywood Reporter, pop music artist Taylor Swift is planning a "secret" theatrical event that will be tied to her upcoming album "The Life of a Showgirl" in collaboration with U.S. theater operator AMC Entertainment (NYSE:AMC). 

AMC shares were up 2% ahead of midday, while shares of other U.S. theater companies had a muted reaction to the report.

The report also said that Swift and her team financed the project with AMC, which cost $15M, and bypassed the traditional Hollywood studio system.

data.title: Lennox declares $1.30 dividend
data.description:

  • Forward yield 0.97%
  • Payable Oct. 15; for shareholders of record Sept. 30; ex-div Sept. 30.

data.title: Nasdaq loses some steam, S&P and Dow cling to gains; Wall Street still set for strong week
data.description:
Wall Street on Friday was largely clinging to gains, though the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) had cooled off a bit after hitting a new record high and the Russell 2000 (RTY) had reversed course after also scaling a new all-time peak.

The S&P 500 (SP500) was last little changed in morning trade. The benchmark gauge was headed for solid weekly gains, though, as market participants finally received an interest rate cut from the Federal Reserve after nearly nine months of inaction on monetary policy. 

The Nasdaq (COMP:IND) was +0.2%, helped by a +1.2% rise in Apple (AAPL). The tech giant's new iPhone 17 became generally available to the public today, with early indicators showing stronger-than-expected demand for the product.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was flat. Meanwhile, the Russell 2000 (RTY) was -0.8%. The benchmark for small-cap stocks on Thursday ended a 967-trading-day streak without a new all-time high, its second longest ever, according to Bespoke Investment Group. 

For traders, the focus on Friday was on a scheduled phone call between U.S. President Donald Trump and China's Xi Jinping. Trump called the conversation "very productive" and agreed to visit Xi in China next year.  

Over in the fixed-income market, yields stabilized somewhat after a two-day bond selloff drove them higher. Despite the Fed's decision to cut its key policy rate by 25 basis points on Wednesday and a more dovish projection on rates for the rest of the year, the selling was ignited by chair Jerome Powell's more hawkish speech. 

"Both the dots and the statement were more dovish than we expected. However, Chair Powell’s press conference was more hawkish. He downplayed the dots and characterized today’s move as a risk management cut, a characterization which casts doubt on whether today’s move is necessarily the start of a long easing cycle," JPMorgan's Michael Feroli said on Wednesday. 

The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was last up 1 basis point to 4.12%, while the more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was little changed at 3.78%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

data.title: Trump looks to visit China next year, Xi to come to U.S.
data.description:
President Donald Trump said he will visit China in the early part of next year, and Chinese President Xi Jinping will head to the U.S. at an appropriate time, after the two presidents ended their scheduled call. 

Trump said he had a "very productive call" with Xi and that they have made progress on many issues, including trade, fentanyl, the war between Russia and Ukraine, and the approval of the TikTok deal. 

"I also agreed with President Xi that we would meet at the APEC Summit in South Korea, that I would go to China in the early part of next year, and that President Xi would, likewise, come to the United States at an appropriate time. The call was a very good one, we will be speaking again by phone, appreciate the TikTok approval, and both look forward to meeting at APEC," Trump said via a post on Truth Social.

Trump and Xi's talks were reportedly "pragmatic, positive, and constructive," and they had a candid and in-depth exchange of views on the current U.S.-China relationship, according to Xinhua. 

On TikTok, the Xinhua report added that China's position remained clear, and the country hopes that the U.S. will have an open, fair and non-discriminatory business environment for Chinese companies looking to invest in the U.S. 

The call was scheduled to start at 9 am ET; however, media reports suggest that it began early at 8 am ET, citing the White House. 

The report from Xinhua added that Xi told Trump that the U.S. should avoid enacting unilateral trade limits. 

The two countries had a lot to cover, some of the topics that are in the forefront include a deal for TikTok, the antitrust investigation by Beijing on Nvidia (NVDA), and the tariff policies. 

The Chinese government said it respects the companies’ choices and welcomes them to engage in commercial negotiations guided by market principles and also look at solutions that are inline with Chinese laws and regulations, according to the Xinhua report.  

On trade, Washington and Beijing had agreed to extend their trade truce for another 90 days in early August after a tit-for-tat tariff battle spiraled out of control.

Potential aircraft orders from Boeing (BA), as well as jet engine components from suppliers like GE Aerospace (GE), Honeywell (HON), and Collins Aerospace (RTX), will likely be discussed, as well as key rare earth elements. 

Here are some China ETFs: (KWEB), (PGJ), (CQQQ), (FXI), (GXC), (MCHI), (FLCH), (CNYA), (ASHR), and (YINN).

data.title: House passes Republican funding bill to avert government shutdown
data.description:
The U.S. House of Representatives on Friday passed a GOP stopgap funding bill to keep the government open until Oct. 1. But the plan now heads to the Senate, where Democrats are expected to reject it in a fight over health care funding.

The House narrowly cleared the Trump-backed legislation in a 217-212 vote, with Democrats almost entirely opposed.

The Republican plan ties in measures such as added security money for lawmakers and federal officials and a clause letting Washington D.C. tap its own tax revenue next year, but otherwise hold spending at current levels with so-called continuing resolution.

The Senate on Friday is expected to vote on both the House version and a competing Democratic proposal from Majority Leader Chuck Schumer to fund the government through Oct. 31.

On Tuesday, though, Democratic leaders warned that they wouldn't support a stop-gap spending bill to avoid a partial government shutdown because it doesn't address their concerns about soaring health insurance coverage costs for millions of Americans. 

Reports said Thursday leaders of the Democratic party are embracing the possibility of a partial U.S. government shutdown on Oct. 1., as they seek to reverse Medicaid cuts that were part of Trump's signature $4T tax and spending cut legislation.

The government's fiscal year ends on Sept. 30.
